title: CNN This Morning · 2026-06-30 · 美国最高法院与出生公民权
summary: 最高法院在任期最后一天发布多项裁决，包括出生公民权、总统解雇独立机构官员权力等。特朗普政府试图通过行政命令终止出生公民权，但面临宪法挑战。同时，科罗拉多州初选中，民主社会主义者挑战现任议员。节目还讨论了联邦储备委员会独立性问题及委内瑞拉地震救援奇迹。

paragraphs.speaker: Cnn Anchor
paragraphs.en: -- Indianapolis International Airport. This was just one of two grenades inside a passenger's checked bag. The passenger was immediately questioned by officials. They haven't said yet if there will be charges. We will continue to track any developments there. That does it for CNN HEADLINE EXPRESS. I'm Brad Smith. CNN THIS MORNING with Audie Cornish starts right now.
paragraphs.cn: -- 印第安纳波利斯国际机场。这只是乘客托运行李中的两枚手榴弹之一。该乘客立即被官员询问。他们尚未说明是否会提出指控。我们将继续追踪任何进展。CNN头条快讯到此结束。我是布拉德·史密斯。奥黛·科尼什主持的《CNN今晨》现在开始。

paragraphs.speaker: Audie Cornish
paragraphs.en: Today in the group chat: the Supreme Court. Will it give Donald Trump more presidential power? And will today's ruling on birthright citizenship be another win for the executive branch?
paragraphs.cn: 今天群聊话题：最高法院。它会赋予特朗普更多总统权力吗？今天关于出生公民权的裁决会是行政部门的又一次胜利吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Female
paragraphs.en: The Supreme Court has said, no, we will not have that independent check. You will not be watchdogs anymore. You will be lapdogs.
paragraphs.cn: 最高法院已经表示，不，我们不会允许那种独立制衡。你们不再是监督者。你们将成为哈巴狗。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Is the Democratic Party tipping to the extreme? What a Michigan Senate candidate says about the party's future.
paragraphs.cn: 民主党是否在走向极端？一位密歇根州参议员候选人对该党未来的看法。

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Male
paragraphs.en: I'm a nobody. I'm just a young man who spent five days trapped beneath a building.
paragraphs.cn: 我是个无名小卒。我只是一个在建筑物下被困五天的年轻人。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Miracles emerge from the devastation in Venezuela. People, even pets being rescued from rubble. DARIALIZA AVILA CHEVALIER (D),
paragraphs.cn: 奇迹从委内瑞拉的废墟中涌现。人们，甚至宠物从瓦砾中被救出。达里亚莉萨·阿维拉·切瓦利埃（民主党），

paragraphs.speaker: New York Congressional Candidate
paragraphs.en: I do regret my tweets. And that's something that I think has brought a lot of division, and that's something I regret.
paragraphs.cn: 我确实后悔我的推文。我认为这带来了很多分裂，这是我后悔的事情。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: A rising star on the left answering for her old social media post. Will her past tweets praising communism be a liability?
paragraphs.cn: 一位左翼新星为她过去的社交媒体帖子辩护。她过去赞扬共产主义的推文会成为负担吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Female
paragraphs.en: The president said it himself. It is the largest expansion of executive power in over 100 years, and that is exactly why we should be worried about it.
paragraphs.cn: 总统自己说过。这是100多年来行政权力最大的一次扩张，这正是我们应该担心的原因。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: The Supreme Court enters the final day of its session today after already giving Donald Trump several key victories and some setbacks. So, where will these final cases fall? Good morning, everybody. I'm Audie Cornish, and it's just a few hours before the Supreme Court will publish their final decisions of this term and then head for summer recess. Expected among those rulings, a key one on birthright citizenship, something the president tried to end with an executive order on his first day in office last year.
paragraphs.cn: 最高法院今天进入会期最后一天，此前已给予唐纳德·特朗普几次关键胜利和一些挫折。那么，这些最终案件将如何裁决？大家早上好。我是奥黛·科尼什，距离最高法院公布本任期最终裁决并进入夏季休会还有几个小时。预计在这些裁决中，有一项关于出生公民权的关键裁决，这是总统去年上任第一天试图通过行政命令终止的。

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Male
paragraphs.en: Birthright citizenship. What are your thoughts? And will you accept it if it rules against you? DONALD TRUMP, U.S. PRESIDENT; Well, I guess I have to accept. It's the Supreme Court, so I'll accept. I think it's very bad for our nation. I don't know. It's up to them. But in terms of for the good of the country, it would be great if they did the -- they didn't allow it.
paragraphs.cn: 出生公民权。你怎么看？如果裁决对你不利，你会接受吗？美国总统唐纳德·特朗普：嗯，我想我必须接受。这是最高法院，所以我会接受。我认为这对我们的国家非常不利。我不知道。这取决于他们。但就国家利益而言，如果他们不允许这样做，那将非常好。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Joining me now in the group chat, Francesca Chambers, White House correspondent for USA Today; Elliot Williams, CNN legal analyst and former federal prosecutor; Chuck Rocha, Democratic strategist and former senior adviser to the Bernie Sanders presidential campaigns; and Ashley Davis, former White House official under President George W. Bush. You guys, thank you so much for being here. Starting with something easy. You know, the Supreme Court.
paragraphs.cn: 现在加入我们群聊的是《今日美国》白宫记者弗朗西斯卡·钱伯斯、CNN法律分析师兼前联邦检察官埃利奥特·威廉姆斯、民主党策略师兼伯尼·桑德斯总统竞选活动前高级顾问查克·罗查，以及乔治·W·布什总统时期的前白宫官员阿什莉·戴维斯。各位，非常感谢你们的到来。从简单的问题开始。你知道，最高法院。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And it's been kind of a weird week for the White House, right? Like, something like mail-in ballots --
paragraphs.cn: 对白宫来说，这周有点奇怪，对吧？比如，像邮寄选票这样的事情 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: -- didn't quite work out. And I want to come to birthright citizenship. But first, can you talk to me about the -- the rejection of the attempt to fire Lisa Cook?
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 并没有完全成功。我想谈谈出生公民权。但首先，你能跟我谈谈关于解雇丽莎·库克的尝试被驳回的事情吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Because this was a weird one.
paragraphs.cn: 因为这很奇怪。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I'm not quite sure what happened in the end.
paragraphs.cn: 我不太确定最后发生了什么。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: I'll tell you exactly why. And they were able -- the court was able to treat the Federal Reserve different from other agencies for a host of different reasons. Something that doesn't get talked a lot about is this idea that the Fed is funded differently than other entities. It's funded, in large part, on account of securities that it holds.
paragraphs.cn: 我来告诉你确切原因。法院能够出于一系列不同的原因，将美联储与其他机构区别对待。一个不常被讨论的问题是，美联储的资金来源与其他实体不同。它的资金主要来自其持有的证券。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: Right? So, they could treat it as not like a typical agency, like the kind that are funded by Congress, and they can carve it out and say that, because of the Fed's unique stature and -- and also, as a policy matter, because the Fed has the power to upend global markets, we have to keep it independent. Now, Amy Coney Barrett noted in a dissent that's kind of bonkers, because they're sort of being a little cute with treating some agencies one way and some another.
paragraphs.cn: 对吧？所以，他们可以不把它当作典型的机构，比如那些由国会资助的机构，他们可以将其单独划分出来，并说，由于美联储独特的地位，而且作为政策问题，因为美联储有能力颠覆全球市场，我们必须保持其独立性。现在，艾米·科尼·巴雷特在异议中指出这有点疯狂，因为他们对待一些机构是一种方式，对待另一些又是另一种方式，这有点耍小聪明。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: Now, in terms of the specifics of Lisa Cook, she was accused of some --
paragraphs.cn: 现在，就丽莎·库克的具体情况而言，她被指控 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Mortgage fraud, yes.
paragraphs.cn: 抵押贷款欺诈，是的。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: -- mortgage fraud and irregularities and was -- like, her termination was related to that. Right? Or at least her purported termination. It's a little different with Rebecca Slaughter over at the Federal Trade Commission, because that was, Can we just fire because we don't want her? Because she doesn't comport with the agency's priorities, with the president's priorities? Pardon me. The Supreme Court yesterday said that's fine.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 抵押贷款欺诈和违规行为，她的解雇与此有关，对吧？或者至少是她声称的解雇。联邦贸易委员会的丽贝卡·斯劳特的情况有点不同，因为问题是：我们能不能仅仅因为不想要她就解雇她？因为她不符合该机构的优先事项，不符合总统的优先事项？抱歉。最高法院昨天说，这没问题。

paragraphs.speaker: Ok. Williams
paragraphs.en: Just the president's ability to fire somebody who disagrees with his -- (CROSSTALK)
paragraphs.cn: 只是总统解雇不同意他观点的人的能力 -- -- （交叉对话）

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: We can fire people that are in independent agencies --
paragraphs.cn: 我们可以解雇独立机构的人 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: -- if we're in the executive branch, except at the Fed, because they've got some special powers.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 如果我们在行政部门，除了美联储，因为他们有一些特殊权力。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: They've got special juice.
paragraphs.cn: 他们有特殊的影响力。

paragraphs.speaker: Ashley Davis
paragraphs.en: My question is, though --
paragraphs.cn: 但我的问题是 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: -- for that, I think that you still have to nominate a Democrat within the party. It still has to be a Democrat nominee. So, her slot wouldn't go to a Republican nominee. It has to still go through Schumer.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 对此，我认为你仍然需要提名党内的一名民主党人。仍然必须是民主党提名人。所以，她的职位不会给共和党提名人。仍然需要经过舒默。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: Well, that's what the law says, but who knows what --
paragraphs.cn: 嗯，法律是这么说的，但谁知道 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: I mean, so, think about it this way. Yes, technically, a Democratic nominee could be put in the FTC or FTC or whatever else. And what stops the president from firing them the next day for -- for whatever reason that that president so chose? According to what the Supreme Court ruled yesterday, presidents now --
paragraphs.cn: 我的意思是，这样想吧。是的，从技术上讲，民主党提名人可以被任命到FTC或其他机构。但什么能阻止总统第二天就解雇他们 -- -- 出于总统选择的任何理由？根据最高法院昨天的裁决，总统现在 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Has the authority, yes.
paragraphs.cn: 拥有这个权力，是的。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: -- have vast authority to remove anybody for any reason that they want.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 拥有广泛的权力，可以以任何理由随意解雇任何人。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: OK. So, there are some reality checks to that power.
paragraphs.cn: 好的。那么，这种权力有一些现实检验。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: People think maybe the birthright citizenship ruling will be one of those reality checks.
paragraphs.cn: 人们认为，或许出生公民权的裁决将成为这些现实检验之一。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Why was this considered a long shot? And I know we've heard the arguments since, so I don't know if there are particular justices you noticed, who expressed skepticism.
paragraphs.cn: 为什么这被认为希望渺茫？我知道我们之后听到了各种论点，所以我不确定你是否注意到有哪位大法官表达了怀疑。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: Right, right. So big picture, the Constitution, 14th Amendment, says that all citizens born in the United States and subject to the jurisdiction thereof, are citizens of the United States. That has been a --
paragraphs.cn: 对，对。那么从大局来看，宪法第十四修正案规定，所有在美国出生并受其管辖的人都是美国公民。这一直是 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And the Trump administration has taken a line we've heard in conservative circles for a long time, which is, hey, that was just an amendment for --
paragraphs.cn: 而特朗普政府采取了一种我们在保守派圈子中早已听过的说法，即，嘿，那只是针对 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: -- the American descendants of slavery.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 美国奴隶的后代。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And it didn't really mean much --
paragraphs.cn: 而且它实际上并不意味着 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: -- for everyone else. So. what is happening? Why --
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 对其他人有什么意义。那么，发生了什么？为什么 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: And moreover, that line, "subject to the jurisdiction thereof," means that it's -- it's a little bit squishier. Now, that is a fringe view in American law. Let's be clear. It's not a widely held view in the legal mainstream. And as we saw at the oral argument in this case, whether it's eight of the justices or seven of the justices, a lot of them are just not sympathetic to the idea.
paragraphs.cn: 此外，"受其管辖"这一表述意味着它有点模糊。现在，这是美国法律中的边缘观点。需要明确的是，这在法律主流中并非广泛持有的观点。正如我们在本案的口头辩论中所见，无论是八位大法官还是七位大法官，其中很多人都对这个想法不以为然。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Oh, that's a big number.
paragraphs.cn: 哦，那是个大数字。

paragraphs.speaker: I -- I -- Cornish
paragraphs.en: We're not looking at, like, one or two.
paragraphs.cn: 我们说的可不是一两个。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: Hard to say and forget, you know -- and Lord strike me down for making a prediction that -- that's a little kooky. But it's just -- just looking at how the oral argument went. You had John Roberts, the chief justice, saying right there in court, Look, the world has changed, but the Constitution has not. Or I think it was same world, different Constitution.
paragraphs.cn: 很难说又很难忘，你知道 -- -- 愿主惩罚我做出这样一个有点古怪的预测。但只是看看口头辩论的情况。首席大法官约翰·罗伯茨在法庭上直接说，看，世界变了，但宪法没变。或者我认为是同一个世界，不同的宪法。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: He is saying that, even though the country might have a different attitude toward immigration today than it did in 1789, the simple fact is, the Constitution is plainly clear on its face, and the administration just has an uphill battle on this one. So, I think they lose. It's just hard to see how much. FRANCESCA CHAMBERS, WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENT, "USA TODAY": Jumping in as the resident White House correspondent on the panel, I think that President Donald Trump knows that.
paragraphs.cn: 他是在说，尽管如今国家对移民的态度可能与1789年不同，但简单的事实是，宪法字面意思很清楚，政府在这件事上处境艰难。所以，我认为他们会输。只是很难看出输多少。《今日美国》白宫记者弗朗西斯卡·钱伯斯：作为小组中的常驻白宫记者，我认为唐纳德·特朗普总统知道这一点。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: You have heard him for months now, attacking justices on the Supreme Court by name, who he thinks are going to potentially rule against him on birthright citizenship; tearing them down over other rulings they've made that have been directly related to tariffs and other issues. But the way that he's been raising birthright citizenship for months in this context suggests that the administration knows, like you said, that this was -- this was a long shot.
paragraphs.cn: 几个月来，你听到他点名攻击最高法院的法官，他认为这些人可能会在出生公民权问题上对他不利；他贬低他们在关税和其他问题上做出的裁决。但他几个月来一直这样提起出生公民权，表明政府知道，就像你说的，这希望渺茫。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: One other thing. Chuck, I want to ask you about the mail-in voting and the voting fight, because in the end, the court seemed to say, look, if things are postmarked by the day, it's fine. But this is still a major sort of talking point on the right. Does it feel like it is something that is helpful for Democrats going forward; that they're not fighting redistricting, mail-in ballot, like, multiple battles?
paragraphs.cn: 还有一件事。查克，我想问你关于邮寄投票和投票斗争的问题，因为最终，法院似乎说，如果东西在当天盖上邮戳，那就没问题。但这仍然是右翼的一个主要话题。这是否感觉对民主党未来有帮助？他们不用同时应对重新划分选区、邮寄选票等多场战斗？

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: I think when you're talking about letting people legally vote, it's a good thing. And I think one of the message points we will have when there was a group text last night talking about this, a lot of these ballots are military ballots. People serving overseas, people that are on ships. This is a big part of why we even created this law --
paragraphs.cn: 我认为当你在谈论让人们合法投票时，这是一件好事。我认为昨晚我们讨论这个问题的群聊中有一个信息点，这些选票很多是军人的选票。在海外服役的人，在船上的人。这是我们制定这项法律的重要原因 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: -- is that when you're overseas and you're military, you should be able to vote in your home district where your house resides. And I think that's a winning message every day when you're letting Americans, especially service members, do your democratic -- democratic duty.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 当你在海外服役时，你应该能够在你家所在的选区投票。我认为这是一个每天都能赢的信息，当你让美国人，特别是军人，履行他们的民主职责时。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Ashley, is there a ruling that you've already seen that you were surprised by, or one you're hoping to hear about today?
paragraphs.cn: 阿什利，有没有你已经看到的让你惊讶的裁决，或者你今天希望听到的裁决？

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: No, I think the transgender athlete issue today will be interesting. I think, on the opposite side, most people think he'll lose the birthright. Most people think he'll win that that the birthright -- the birth. I'm sorry, the transgender.
paragraphs.cn: 没有，我认为今天关于跨性别运动员的问题会很有趣。我认为，另一方面，大多数人认为他会在出生公民权上输。大多数人认为他会赢那个出生 -- -- 出生。抱歉，是跨性别。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: State bans on transgender athletes in youth sports.
paragraphs.cn: 各州禁止跨性别运动员参加青少年体育比赛。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Exactly. What's interesting to me were the dissents yesterday. So, you have, like, Roberts, who dissented at least twice. I mean, in regards to the two FTC and the Reserve. And then Brett Kavanaugh --
paragraphs.cn: 没错。让我感兴趣的是昨天的异议意见。比如，罗伯茨至少两次提出异议。我是说，关于联邦贸易委员会和储备委员会的两个案子。然后布雷特·卡瓦诺 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: -- actually was one of the dissents for -- he sided with Roberts and the minority.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 实际上是异议者之一 -- -- 他站在罗伯茨和少数派一边。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: And then Amy Coney Barrett, who does a lot, do the --
paragraphs.cn: 然后是艾米·科尼·巴雷特，她做了很多 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And who I've already seen as a kind of target in sort of right-wing media circles.
paragraphs.cn: 而且我已经看到她在右翼媒体圈中成为某种目标。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: Yes. I think a couple things. Just in American history, always read the dissents, because that's where you're seeing sort of what the future of American law is.
paragraphs.cn: 是的。我认为有几点。在美国历史上，总是要读异议意见，因为你能从中看到美国法律的未来走向。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. Just ask Clarence Thomas.
paragraphs.cn: 没错。问问克拉伦斯·托马斯就知道了。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: Ask Clarence Thomas. And where they're disagreeing. But also, the big fight yesterday was Amy Coney Barrett versus Justice Alito over terminations and firings. And there was just -- you're watching fights among the conservatives, which are really fascinating.
paragraphs.cn: 问问克拉伦斯·托马斯。看看他们在哪些方面存在分歧。而且，昨天的大战是艾米·科尼·巴雷特与阿利托大法官关于解雇和开除的争论。你看到的是保守派之间的争斗，这真的很有趣。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. The seeds of discontent become law at some point.
paragraphs.cn: 是的。不满的种子在某个时候会变成法律。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: We were reading her dissent that's, like, 80 billion pages that happened yesterday, top.
paragraphs.cn: 我们昨天读了她那篇长达800亿页的异议意见，真是长篇大论。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Hey, you guys wanted on camera, too. So, we'll see. All right, you guys, stay with me. We've got a lot to talk about this hour coming up on CNN THIS MORNING. Today is primary day in Colorado. There's a Gen Z congressional candidate taking on an incumbent who has elected -- who was elected the year before she was born. So, are we looking at a generational shift? Plus, a World Cup knockout. Paraguay stuns Germany, defeating the four-time world champions.
paragraphs.cn: 嘿，你们也想上镜头。那我们等着瞧。好了，各位，跟我来。本期《CNN今晨》要讨论的话题很多。今天是科罗拉多州的初选日。一位Z世代国会候选人挑战一位在她出生前就已当选的现任议员。那么，我们是否正目睹一场代际更迭？此外，世界杯爆冷：巴拉圭击败四届世界冠军德国队。

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Male
paragraphs.en: A few have been in this position for Paraguay, but he scores! Paraguay have done it! Paraguay won it! MELAT KIROS (D),
paragraphs.cn: 巴拉圭队曾有几次机会，但他进球了！巴拉圭做到了！巴拉圭赢了！梅拉特·基罗斯（民主党）：

paragraphs.speaker: Colorado Congressional Candidate
paragraphs.en: Experience only matters if you actually do something with it. And we're finding now with a lot of incumbent Democrats, that they're not actually able to deliver meaningfully on the things that working families need right now.
paragraphs.cn: 经验只有在付诸行动时才有意义。我们现在发现，许多现任民主党议员实际上无法切实满足工薪家庭当前的需求。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, it's primary day in Colorado. Democrats are eyeing one particular congressional district, which sees a 30-year-long incumbent, Diana Degette, fighting for her seat against 29-year-old lawyer and Democratic socialist Melat Kiros. Degette took over in office the year that Kiros was born. Democratic socialists have successfully advanced in primaries in New York City, Washington, D.C., and L.A. Now, establishment Democrats are wondering if this wave will crash into Colorado today. The group chat is back.
paragraphs.cn: 今天是科罗拉多州的初选日。民主党人正关注一个特别的国会选区：连任30年的现任议员戴安娜·德盖特正与29岁的律师、民主社会主义者梅拉特·基罗斯争夺席位。德盖特上任那年，基罗斯刚出生。民主社会主义者已在纽约市、华盛顿特区和洛杉矶的初选中成功推进。如今，建制派民主党人想知道这股浪潮今天是否会席卷科罗拉多州。群聊又开始了。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I want to start with my Democratic adviser here.
paragraphs.cn: 我想先听听我的民主党顾问的意见。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Why is Colorado close? I have to admit, I looked at Colorado as a place where the Democrats who appealed in purple states had sort of made a home and were showing they could do it. And yet even Michael Bennet, the senator, people are looking at his seat now and be like, well, wait a second, what's going on? So, why are they vulnerable there?
paragraphs.cn: 为什么科罗拉多州竞争激烈？我得承认，我曾认为科罗拉多州是那些在摇摆州有吸引力的民主党人安家并证明自己能行的地方。然而，就连参议员迈克尔·贝内特，人们现在也在关注他的席位，心想：等等，怎么回事？那么，为什么他们在那里处境脆弱？

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: People keep forgetting that Colorado is one of those states that 20 years ago was a purple state, almost a red state.
paragraphs.cn: 人们总是忘记，科罗拉多州是20年前还是紫色州、几乎接近红色州的州之一。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: And it's become more and more blue as you've had lots of migration into there. But what you're --
paragraphs.cn: 随着大量人口迁入，它变得越来越蓝。但你所看到的 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: And it's been periwinkle.
paragraphs.cn: 而且它一直是淡蓝色。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: But what you see there is you see this inside versus outside game.
paragraphs.cn: 但你在那里看到的是内部与外部博弈。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: This is not much about Democratic socialists against conservatives or however the media or operatives like me want to categorize it. But it's about you see these incumbents, and here's the pattern that's emerged even from New York to Colorado, is you have lots of really safe Democrats I've talked about on this show. Lots of Democrats have very safe congressional seats, just like lots of Republicans do.
paragraphs.cn: 这与其说是民主社会主义者与保守派之间的对立，或者像我这样的媒体或操作人员想要归类的那样，不如说，你看到这些现任议员，从纽约到科罗拉多都出现了一种模式，那就是有很多非常安全的民主党人，我在这个节目里谈到过。很多民主党人拥有非常安全的国会席位，就像很多共和党人一样。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: And what that causes you to do is not spend any money talking to voters over a long period of time, because you're safe cycle after cycle.
paragraphs.cn: 这导致的结果是，你不需要花任何钱长期与选民沟通，因为你一个又一个周期都很安全。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: And when you have these insurgent candidates where there's money being spent in a time where incumbents are looked at as poison, Democrat or Republican.
paragraphs.cn: 而当这些挑战者候选人出现时，在现任议员被视为毒药的时候，无论是民主党还是共和党，都有资金投入。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: You see an insurgency ready to happen.
paragraphs.cn: 你会看到一场叛乱即将发生。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So "Politico" is writing about Degette, and one of these on background types said it's not looking great. And it's very tough when you're fighting against a wave. And getting to that word, you're talking about, the wave. I think the thing that I wrestle with is that there are so many, both progressives and even, let's say, Congressional Black Caucus has this problem.
paragraphs.cn: 所以《政治报》正在报道德吉特，其中一位匿名人士说情况不太乐观。当你对抗一股浪潮时，这非常艰难。说到这个词，你提到的浪潮。我认为我纠结的是，有太多人，无论是进步派，甚至比如说，国会黑人核心小组也有这个问题。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Latino Caucus has this problem. These guys have been in office for so long, but they only just now are starting to get gavels and be in power. And they're, like, trying to turn to the voter and be like, no, no, no, no, no, no. We just -- we're going to do it now.
paragraphs.cn: 拉丁裔核心小组也有这个问题。这些人已经在位很久了，但他们直到现在才开始获得权力和领导职位。他们试图转向选民说，不，不，不，不，不，不。我们只是 -- -- 我们现在就要做。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: It's important to say this. Just one second. Degette, who she's running against, is in the Progressive Caucus.
paragraphs.cn: 这一点很重要，稍等一下。德吉特的对手是进步核心小组的成员。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: She's a very progressive member.
paragraphs.cn: 她是一位非常进步的议员。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: She just hasn't spent any money talking to voters in a long time.
paragraphs.cn: 她只是很久没有花钱与选民沟通了。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: She also has a really reasonable -- I mean, like I know from the Republican side, for her and Bennet, Republicans like to work with both of them. So that's a problem right there when they're in this election. But I agree with you. Everyone's too old, and everyone is just tired of the status quo.
paragraphs.cn: 她还有一个非常合理的 -- -- 我是说，从共和党方面我知道，对于她和贝内特，共和党人喜欢与他们两人合作。所以在这场选举中，这本身就是一个问题。但我同意你的看法。每个人都太老了，每个人都厌倦了现状。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. And I'm the first to be out here talking about gerontocracy. I'm not going to act as though I've been immune to this language, but I have wrestled with it as I've thought about, like, some of these -- again, the progressives, the black, Latino. There's -- there's some affinity groups in Congress where they really never got into positions of power until now, right? When people are like, well, wait a second. You're too old to do the job.
paragraphs.cn: 是的。而且我是第一个站出来谈论老人政治的人。我不会假装自己不受这种说法的影响，但我在思考这些问题时确实在纠结 -- -- 比如，那些进步派、黑人、拉丁裔。国会里有一些利益团体，他们直到现在才真正获得权力地位，对吧？当人们说，等等，你太老了，干不了这份工作。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: Yes. The other -- the other factor looming over this is that you saw this with Republicans in 2012 and 2016, that when a party is out of power and sort of in the wilderness, people are even hungrier to sort of, as both of you were saying, to sort of upset the world and see a lot of the older --
paragraphs.cn: 是的。另一个笼罩着的问题在于，你在2012年和2016年的共和党人身上看到过，当一个政党失去权力、处于荒野之中时，人们甚至更渴望 -- -- 正如你们两位所说 -- -- 颠覆世界，看到许多年长的 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes, because why not? You're already in the wilderness.
paragraphs.cn: 是的，为什么不呢？反正已经在荒野里了。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: Why not? Why not, we're in the wilderness.
paragraphs.cn: 为什么不呢？为什么不呢，我们在荒野里。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: And -- and also we -- various factions have different visions for how the future ought to be. And they're very, very different. Again, you saw it with the Republicans. And that led, in some part, to the Tea Party, but also to Donald Trump.
paragraphs.cn: 而且 -- -- 而且我们 -- -- 不同的派系对未来应该是什么样子有不同的愿景。它们非常、非常不同。再说一次，你在共和党人身上看到了这一点。这在一定程度上导致了茶党，但也导致了唐纳德·特朗普。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Can I talk about this split?
paragraphs.cn: 我能谈谈这种分裂吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: -- what the party was.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 这个政党曾经是什么。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Let me talk about the split divisions for a minute, because where this surfaces over and over again is this counting of how many socialists may be coming. We are panicking about that. This is our manifesto. Senator Chris Coons was talking about this on Friday. Here was his take. And we hear this take often. SEN. CHRIS COONS (D-DE): I'm a member of the Democratic Party. I'm not a member of the Socialist Party.
paragraphs.cn: 让我谈谈分裂的派系，因为这个问题反复出现，就是关于有多少社会主义者可能到来的统计。我们对此感到恐慌。这是我们的宣言。参议员克里斯·库恩斯周五谈到了这一点。以下是他的看法。我们经常听到这种看法。参议员克里斯·库恩斯（特拉华州民主党）：我是民主党的一员。我不是社会党的一员。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And frankly, I think that, while folks can take ideas from other political traditions, if you're running in a Democratic primary, you ought to be a Democrat.
paragraphs.cn: 坦率地说，我认为，虽然人们可以从其他政治传统中汲取思想，但如果你参加民主党初选，你就应该是民主党人。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And then in the meantime, poor Hakeem Jeffries --
paragraphs.cn: 与此同时，可怜的哈基姆·杰弗里斯 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: -- has to be asked about this over and over again. He is, as we say, House minority leader, hoping to change that in November. And people are saying, are you worried about the fall? Here's what he had to say on Monday.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 不得不反复被问到这个问题。正如我们所说，他是众议院少数党领袖，希望在11月改变这一局面。人们都在问，你担心秋季的选举吗？以下是他在周一所说的话。

paragraphs.speaker: Manu Raju
paragraphs.en: Mr. Jeffries, do you think that the -- all these socialists who are coming in here, is that good for your party right now? REP. HAKEEM JEFFRIES (D-NY): The party is going to continue to focus on winning seats currently held by Republicans and flipping them blue.
paragraphs.cn: 杰弗里斯先生，你认为所有这些社会主义者涌入这里，对你们党现在有好处吗？众议员哈基姆·杰弗里斯（纽约州民主党）：本党将继续专注于赢得目前由共和党占据的席位，并将其翻蓝。

paragraphs.speaker: Raju
paragraphs.en: Yes, but they're going to come in, and they're going to demand the moon, and you're not going to be able to deliver the moon.
paragraphs.cn: 是的，但他们来了之后会要求天上的月亮，而你根本摘不到月亮。

paragraphs.speaker: Jeffries
paragraphs.en: Well, let's cross that bridge when we get to it, because I think, before we can even get to governing, we have to win.
paragraphs.cn: 好吧，船到桥头自然直，因为我认为，在考虑执政之前，我们必须先赢得选举。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Pro tip: when they walk slow, they want to answer.
paragraphs.cn: 小贴士：当他们走得很慢时，说明他们想回答问题。

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.en: This is part of, you know, this broader debate that's taking place in the Democratic Party right now, leading into not just the midterm elections. I know that you hate it when I talk about 2028, Audie.
paragraphs.cn: 这是民主党内部目前正在进行的更广泛辩论的一部分，不仅涉及中期选举。我知道你不喜欢我谈论2028年，奥迪。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: No, I'm there. I'm there. We're doing it. We're doing it as it comes.
paragraphs.cn: 不，我准备好了。我准备好了。我们正在做。我们顺其自然。

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.en: OK. But leading into the 2028 presidential race is the -- the country determines what it wants after Donald Trump, who is term- limited and whether or not the Democratic Party, you know, fighting against Donald Trump is enough. Or if there has to be some other set of policies and ideas. And if so, what exactly those are. You see that playing out in New York, in Colorado, and across the country right now.
paragraphs.cn: 好的。但在2028年总统大选之前，国家要决定在任期受限的特朗普之后想要什么，以及民主党与特朗普斗争是否足够。或者是否需要其他一系列政策和理念。如果需要，具体是什么。你现在在纽约、科罗拉多以及全国都能看到这种情况。

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.en: But it's not just, by the way, on the Democratic side, although I know that's what we're talking about right now. And I think this is what you were about to get into --
paragraphs.cn: 但顺便说一句，这不仅仅发生在民主党这边，尽管我知道我们现在讨论的是这个。而且我认为你正要谈到 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. We didn't even get into the Republican side of the Colorado race.
paragraphs.cn: 是的。我们甚至还没谈到科罗拉多州竞选中的共和党一方。

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.en: I see this happening on the Republican side, as well, with the country. Republicans, at least in this country, repeatedly siding with President Donald Trump when he backs candidates over people who are establishment, if he simply says that this person wasn't supportive enough.
paragraphs.cn: 我也看到共和党这边也在发生同样的情况。至少在这个国家，共和党人一再站在唐纳德·特朗普总统一边，当他支持候选人而非建制派时，只要他说这个人不够支持他。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Republicans in the primaries.
paragraphs.cn: 共和党初选。

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.en: In the primaries. In the primaries.
paragraphs.cn: 在初选中。在初选中。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And for both parties, the question is the general.
paragraphs.cn: 对两党来说，问题在于大选。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: But it's also -- it's also important to note we're talking about blue district primaries in blue districts, in blue states. These are --
paragraphs.cn: 但同样重要的是，我们谈论的是蓝色选区、蓝色州的蓝色选区初选。这些是 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: But let me push back.
paragraphs.cn: 但让我反驳一下。

paragraphs.speaker: Williams
paragraphs.en: -- you know, this is not the entire country.
paragraphs.cn: -- -- 你知道，这并非整个国家的情况。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: Let me push back tonight. In -- in Colorado Eight tonight, in this race today that they're having in Colorado, it's a true marginal seat that we're talking about. It's one of those 40 seats that is literally 50/50. It's just North of downtown. My PAC, in full disclosure, has run some ads there for a Latino that's running, a young Latino who's 31, running against a sitting state senator there. She's 57. Between the two Democrats. Also, there's a freshman Republican.
paragraphs.cn: 让我今晚反驳一下。在科罗拉多第八选区今晚，在今天他们进行的这场竞选中，我们谈论的是一个真正的边缘席位。这是那40个真正五五开的席位之一。就在市中心以北。我的政治行动委员会，完全公开地说，在那里为一位参选的拉丁裔候选人投放了一些广告，他31岁，年轻拉丁裔，与一位现任州参议员竞争。她57岁。两人都是民主党人。此外，还有一位新晋共和党人。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: This seat will determine who controls Congress and many seats just like that.
paragraphs.cn: 这个席位将决定谁控制国会，许多席位都像这样。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Who's going to win that one? The -- the --
paragraphs.cn: 谁会赢那个席位？ -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: The young guy's probably going to win it.
paragraphs.cn: 那个年轻人很可能会赢。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Well, don't ask him, will you put money on the race? But also, sir, you're saying 51 is old?
paragraphs.cn: 好吧，别问他，你会押注这场比赛吗？但是，先生，您是说51岁算老吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: I'm saying in this context it is.
paragraphs.cn: 我是说在这种情况下算老。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: It's a generational change.
paragraphs.cn: 这是代际更替。

paragraphs.speaker: Ok. Rocha
paragraphs.en: Fifty-one is too old.
paragraphs.cn: 51岁太老了。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Hope and change. Chuck Rocha, Elliot, thank you for being here. I love when you enter the chat. After the break on CNN THIS MORNING from breaking NFL records to fighting for his life. Chris Johnson reveals his life-changing diagnosis. Plus, a dangerous heat wave set up across the Eastern half of the U.S., taking you all the way into the July 4th holiday weekend. And Washington, D.C., expected to top out Saturday in the triple digits, forecasted to have its hottest Fourth of July on record.
paragraphs.cn: 希望与变革。查克·罗查，艾略特，感谢你们的到来。我喜欢你们加入讨论。休息过后，CNN《今晨》节目将从打破NFL纪录到为生命而战。克里斯·约翰逊透露了他改变人生的诊断。此外，一场危险的热浪席卷美国东半部，一直持续到7月4日假期周末。华盛顿特区预计周六气温将突破三位数，有望成为有记录以来最热的7月4日。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: It's now 25 minutes past the hour. This is your morning roundup: some of the stories you might have missed. Did you know there are survivors still being found in Venezuela after last night's earthquakes? Crews rescued a 12-year-old who was trapped underneath the rubble. Now, this came hours after a 21-year-old man was pulled from underneath a building that collapsed. He'd been trapped for days. And here's what he had to say after the rescue. AARON LEVI CANTILLO, RESCUED MAN (through translator): I'm a nobody.
paragraphs.cn: 现在是整点过25分。这是你的早间新闻汇总：一些你可能错过的故事。你知道吗，昨晚委内瑞拉地震后仍有幸存者被找到？救援人员救出了一名被困在废墟下的12岁儿童。而在此之前数小时，一名21岁男子从倒塌的建筑下被救出。他被困了数天。以下是他在获救后所说的话。获救男子亚伦·莱维·坎蒂略（通过翻译）：我是个无名小卒。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I'm just a young man who spent five days trapped beneath a building. But I'm here today, and I'm alive, thanks to my God. He is the only one who got me out of there and sent his angels. Who were his angels? The people who worked tirelessly on my rescue. The people who cared about how I was doing, the people who were there when I came out, who shouted my name when they saw me emerge. As far as I know, I was just informed that I was the only person in that building who was found alive.
paragraphs.cn: 我只是一个被困在建筑下五天的年轻人。但我今天在这里，我还活着，感谢我的上帝。他是唯一一个把我从那里救出来并派来天使的人。谁是他的天使？那些为我的救援不懈努力的人，那些关心我状况的人，那些在我出来时在场的人，那些看到我出现时呼喊我名字的人。据我所知，我被告知我是那栋建筑中唯一被发现活着的人。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: In the meantime, there are crews finding pets barking from beneath the damage, which led them to pull this puppy to safety. Now, these are only some of the lucky few who have been found alive. The death toll currently stands at around 1,700; 5,000 people injured. Many more remain missing. Former NFL star running back Chris Johnson revealed in a TV interview that he suffers from ALS. The 40-year-old spent ten years in the NFL and last played in 2017.
paragraphs.cn: 与此同时，救援人员发现宠物在废墟下吠叫，从而将这只小狗救出。这些只是少数幸运的幸存者。目前死亡人数约为1700人，5000人受伤。还有更多人失踪。前NFL明星跑卫克里斯·约翰逊在接受电视采访时透露他患有肌萎缩侧索硬化症（ALS）。这位40岁的球员在NFL效力了十年，最后一次比赛是在2017年。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Now, in his interview on ABC's "Good Morning America," he was only able to communicate through a computerized speech generation device.
paragraphs.cn: 在ABC《早安美国》的采访中，他只能通过计算机语音生成设备进行交流。

paragraphs.speaker: Chris Johnson
paragraphs.en: I first noticed weakness in my right hand. At first it was little things like my grip didn't feel right, and I wasn't as strong as I've always been. It's continued to progress much faster than I ever imagined.
paragraphs.cn: 我最初注意到右手无力。起初是一些小事，比如握力感觉不对，也不像以前那样强壮。但病情进展的速度远超我的想象。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Now, Johnson is one of only nine players in NFL history to rush more than 2,000 yards in a season. And Paraguay shocked Germany in a massive upset last night on penalties.
paragraphs.cn: 如今，约翰逊是NFL历史上仅有的九位单赛季冲球超过2000码的球员之一。而巴拉圭在昨晚的点球大战中爆冷击败德国，震惊了世界。

paragraphs.speaker: Unidentified Male
paragraphs.en: Down to Jose Canale. A few have been in this position for Paraguay, but he scores! Paraguay have done it! Paraguay have won it! And they've booted Germany out of the World Cup!
paragraphs.cn: 轮到何塞·卡纳莱。巴拉圭队此前有几人曾站在这个位置，但他进球了！巴拉圭做到了！巴拉圭赢了！他们淘汰了德国队，将其踢出世界杯！

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Woof. The four-time World Cup winners now out of this year's championships. It's the first time the Germans have ever lost in the World Cup on penalties. Paraguay advancing to the Round of 16 later this week. And straight ahead on CNN THIS MORNING, deleted tweets from a rising Democratic figure now raising questions about socialist views inside the party. Plus, what a key vote on military aid to Israel might say about deep divisions inside the party, as well. var CNN = CNN || {};
paragraphs.cn: 哇。四届世界杯冠军如今止步本届赛事。这是德国队首次在世界杯点球大战中失利。巴拉圭将在本周晚些时候晋级16强。接下来在CNN《今晨》节目中，一位民主党新星删除的推文引发了对党内社会主义观点的质疑。此外，一项对以色列军事援助的关键投票也揭示了党内的深刻分歧。

paragraphs.speaker: Audie Cornish
paragraphs.en: A CNN KFILE investigation uncovered deleted social media posts from Democratic congressional nominee Darializa Avila Chevalier's old Twitter account. So, the posts include favorable references to communism, Marxist ideology, and authoritarian communist figures. She says she has grown considerably since those posts. And when asked about President Trump accusing her of being a communist, she said this. DARIALIZA AVILA CHEVALIER (D),
paragraphs.cn: CNN KFILE调查发现，民主党国会候选人达里亚利扎·阿维拉·切瓦利尔的旧推特账户中删除了社交媒体帖子。这些帖子包含对共产主义、马克思主义意识形态以及威权共产主义人物的正面提及。她表示自那些帖子发布以来，她已经成长了很多。当被问及特朗普总统指责她是共产主义者时，她这样回应。达里亚利扎·阿维拉·切瓦利尔（民主党）：

paragraphs.speaker: U.S. House Candidate From New York
paragraphs.en: You know, I think that is -- that framing is one that I've been very proud to be able to say I don't respond to. One in which I have been very intentional to say, I won't be reactive.
paragraphs.cn: 你知道，我认为这种框架是我非常自豪能够说我不回应的。我一直有意地说，我不会做出反应。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Group chat is back. I wanted to talk about this for two reasons. One, the tweets. This is a second round of going through her social media posts and finding politically unsavory things. Before we get to that, her saying, I'm just not going to react, is that going to work when you think of the consultants that are out there writing their ads right now, with just pictures of Marx and Lenin and a stamp over her face?
paragraphs.cn: 群聊又回来了。我想谈这个有两个原因。一是这些推文。这是第二轮翻查她的社交媒体帖子，发现政治上不受欢迎的内容。在此之前，她说她只是不会做出反应，当你想到那些正在撰写广告的顾问们，他们现在就用马克思和列宁的照片，并在她脸上盖个章，这能行吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Chuck Rocha
paragraphs.en: Two things can happen at the same time. She can regret those tweets. She can say that they were stupid. She can not respond to them, like she did. But I'll promise you, there is a Republican out there now taking those tweets and going to put them on an ad that says all Democrats are this way.
paragraphs.cn: 两件事可以同时发生。她可以后悔那些推文。她可以说那些推文很愚蠢。她可以像她那样不予回应。但我向你保证，现在有一个共和党人正在拿那些推文，把它们做成广告，说所有民主党人都这样。

paragraphs.speaker: Ashley Davis
paragraphs.en: But for her seat, it doesn't matter. I mean she's going to win no matter what.
paragraphs.cn: 但对她所在的选区来说，这并不重要。我的意思是，无论如何她都会赢。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: So, I would think, in her -- in her situation --
paragraphs.cn: 所以，我认为，在她的 -- -- 在她的情况下 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Well, here's just a sample. Here is President Trump, how he plans on talking about it.
paragraphs.cn: 嗯，这里只是举个例子。这是特朗普总统打算如何谈论这件事。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Right, but for her situation --
paragraphs.cn: 对，但就她的情况而言 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: On Monday, this is how he talked about the whole socialism thing.
paragraphs.cn: 周一，他是这样谈论整个社会主义问题的。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Yes. Not responding.
paragraphs.cn: 是的。没有回应。

paragraphs.speaker: Donald Trump
paragraphs.en: It's not socialism. It's really communism. They use the word social Democrat because it sounds so nice, but it's really communism you're talking about. I think it's the biggest threat to our nation there is, maybe since our founding. That includes World War I, World War II, September 11th. It includes the Pearl Harbor attack.
paragraphs.cn: 这不是社会主义。这实际上是共产主义。他们用社会民主党这个词，因为听起来很好听，但你谈的实际上是共产主义。我认为这是我国自建国以来面临的最大威胁，甚至包括第一次世界大战、第二次世界大战、9·11事件，还有珍珠港袭击。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, it's not whether or not she can -- this blue state or this blue district will vote for a person who's in their leaning. It's, when you paint the broad brush, how far does it reach and does it stick?
paragraphs.cn: 所以，问题不在于她能否 -- -- 这个蓝州或蓝区是否会投票给一个倾向他们的人。而是，当你大笔一挥时，这影响能波及多远，能否站得住脚？

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Well, I think that -- and we were talking about this in the break -- that Donald Trump kind of normalized some of the things that you've said. And people seem to either want to care or not want to care. And they --
paragraphs.cn: 嗯，我认为 -- -- 我们休息时也在讨论 -- -- 唐纳德·特朗普某种程度上让你说的那些话变得正常化了。人们似乎要么在乎，要么不在乎。而且他们 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: On social media specifically, yes.
paragraphs.cn: 特别是在社交媒体上，是的。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: On social media in general. But I have to say, she grew up, I would think, I don't know exactly how old she is, where --
paragraphs.cn: 总的来说是在社交媒体上。但我必须说，她是在 -- -- 我想，我不确定她具体多大 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: She's young, yes, Through this whole social media world, where you just put your whole life out there.
paragraphs.cn: 她很年轻，是的。在这个社交媒体世界里，你把自己的整个生活都放在上面。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: And so, I do think times are changing. But as I also was saying, like, in the business community, it's also changing. I threaten my son every day not to put things, which is probably why the disappearing apps are more popular than ever, because you can't go back and find those as easy.
paragraphs.cn: 所以，我确实认为时代在变。但正如我所说，商界也在变化。我每天都威胁儿子不要乱放东西，这大概就是为什么那些阅后即焚的应用比以往更受欢迎，因为你无法轻易找回那些内容。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: But you're saying we're at an age where it would be hard pressed to find someone in their 30s at this point --
paragraphs.cn: 但你是说，我们现在这个时代，很难找到一个30多岁的人 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Correct. You live your life on this. I mean, yes.
paragraphs.cn: 没错。你的生活就靠这个。我是说，是的。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Who doesn't have a social media background worth scrubbing. FRANCESCA CHAMBERS, WHITE HOUSE CORRESPONDENT, "USA TODAY": Why are you looking at me?
paragraphs.cn: 谁没有一段值得清理的社交媒体历史呢？《今日美国》白宫记者弗朗西斯卡·钱伯斯：你为什么看着我？

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: You're a person who may.
paragraphs.cn: 你是一个可能有（这种历史）的人。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: You're the (INAUDIBLE) of us.
paragraphs.cn: 你是我们当中（听不清）的人。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I don't -- a young person. No, seriously, I wrestle with this because also it's -- I would think it's fair game. If Democrats can go scrubbing for old tweets where you find out there are Republican candidates who have said things that are racist, doesn't it -- isn't it fair game to go after somebody who has said, abolish police, or said things that actually are even directly related to policy?
paragraphs.cn: 我不是 -- -- 一个年轻人。不，说真的，我对此很纠结，因为这也是 -- -- 我认为这是公平的游戏。如果民主党人可以翻找旧推文，发现共和党候选人说过种族主义的话，那么去追究那些说过"废除警察"或直接与政策相关的话的人，难道不是公平的游戏吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.en: I think regardless of what are in these social media posts from any candidate, that President Donald Trump and the Republican Party are going to make this argument about these Democratic socialists as alleged communists one way or another.
paragraphs.cn: 我认为，无论任何候选人的社交媒体帖子内容如何，唐纳德·特朗普总统和共和党都会以某种方式将这些民主社会主义者称为所谓的共产主义者。

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.en: I mean, he's made it, you know, very clear, going back to his comments on Friday, when he was at a religious conservative conference and he spent most of his speech, by the way, talking about this, talking about the threat of communism, explaining about how Democrats are going to try and offer you free stuff. And that might sound good for a couple of years.
paragraphs.cn: 我的意思是，他已经说得很清楚了，回顾他周五的评论，当时他在一个宗教保守派会议上，顺便说一句，他大部分演讲都在谈论这个，谈论共产主义的威胁，解释民主党如何试图给你免费的东西。这可能在头几年听起来不错。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Do you think it's weird they're skipping straight to communism? Like isn't -- I just want to show, positive opinions of socialism. In 2010, yes, there's movement from Democrats. Democrats now view socialism more favorably. But Americans in general don't view it all that much better than they did in 2010.
paragraphs.cn: 你觉得他们直接跳到共产主义很奇怪吗？比如，我只是想展示一下对社会主义的正面看法。2010年，是的，民主党人有所转变。民主党人现在对社会主义的看法更积极。但总体而言，美国人对此的看法并不比2010年好多少。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And I just wonder why you can't just critique socialism as is, and you have to go straight to communism.
paragraphs.cn: 我只是好奇，为什么你不能直接批评社会主义本身，而非要扯到共产主义。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Communism is much more, if you're just branding them, and you know this as a communicator for campaigns, it's much easier to understand.
paragraphs.cn: 共产主义更甚，如果你只是给他们贴标签，而你知道作为竞选沟通者，这更容易理解。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: (INAUDIBLE) to go straight back to the '50s. (CROSS TALK)
paragraphs.cn: （听不清）直接回到50年代。（交叉对话）

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Love it. OK. Well --
paragraphs.cn: 喜欢。好的。那么 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: Absolutely. And what we come down to in this is, in a -- in a time in a midterm, for all of you at home, national polls and the way the sentiment doesn't matter. It only matters in a handful of congressional races, none of which that are in the inside of any major city. They're all in the exurbs or the suburbs. So, you're talking to a group -- different group of people than you would in a downtown urban setting.
paragraphs.cn: 当然。归根结底，在中期选举期间，对于在家的各位来说，全国民调和舆论走向并不重要。重要的只是少数几个国会选区的竞争，而这些选区都不在任何大城市的中心，全都在远郊或近郊。所以，你是在和一群不同于市中心环境的人对话。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: And the real basis comes down to, do you care more about somebody's regretful tweet or the price of hamburger and eggs? That's what it's going to come down to.
paragraphs.cn: 真正的根本问题在于，你更在意某人的一条令人遗憾的推文，还是汉堡和鸡蛋的价格？这才是关键。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Well, also, in a new media market. I'm going to turn to another topic before I go. Hasan Piker, the streamer, Democratic activist, has been backing a lot of Democratic socialist candidates. He said this about the idea of going into people's background and want to play it for you guys.
paragraphs.cn: 另外，在新的媒体市场。在我结束之前，我要转向另一个话题。主播、民主党活动家哈桑·派克一直支持许多民主社会主义候选人。他谈到挖掘候选人背景的想法，我想放给你们听。

paragraphs.speaker: Hasan Piker
paragraphs.en: If you are advocating or advancing society in a direction that capital owners don't want, they're going to come after you. They're going to come after you. And if they can't find anything in your background, they're going to invent stuff, right? We saw this with Nadya Alyssa (ph). They didn't have anything that they can hit her on, so they just started, you know, they just started creating controversy off of her tweets. This is how these guys operate.
paragraphs.cn: 如果你在倡导或推动社会朝着资本所有者不想要的方向发展，他们就会来找你。他们会来找你。如果他们在你的背景里找不到任何东西，他们就会编造，对吧？我们在纳迪亚·阿莉莎（音）身上看到了这一点。他们没有任何可以攻击她的点，所以他们就开始，你知道，他们就开始利用她的推文制造争议。这些人就是这么操作的。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: He was talking about oppositional research. He was -- you're nodding.
paragraphs.cn: 他是在说反对派研究。他 -- -- 你在点头。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Yes, I mean, this is -- if you're going to run, everything is fair game. I mean this is why Chuck and I are never able to run for office.
paragraphs.cn: 是的，我的意思是，如果你要参选，一切都是公平的。这就是为什么查克和我永远无法竞选公职的原因。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Well, let me state here --
paragraphs.cn: 那么，让我在这里说明 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: I mean this is like as old as time in regards to the -- everything's fair game. This is what you're putting yourself out there for.
paragraphs.cn: 我的意思是，这就像老生常谈 -- -- 一切都是公平的。这就是你把自己置于风口浪尖的原因。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: All right. Well, another thing that's become fair game, and the reason why I wanted to play Piker is this conversation about Israel, which continues to divide the party, particularly the Democratic Party. Chevalier's opinions on that have also come up. Speaking on this sort of shifting landscape with Mayor Mamdani, who backed her, actually being asked to clarify his position on Israel. And here's what he had to say.
paragraphs.cn: 好吧。另一件成为焦点的事情，也是我想播放皮克的原因，就是关于以色列的对话，这个话题继续分裂着政党，尤其是民主党。舍瓦利耶对此的观点也被提及。谈到这种不断变化的局势，支持她的马姆达尼市长实际上被要求澄清他对以色列的立场。以下是他所说的。

paragraphs.speaker: Jonathan Karl
paragraphs.en: And the idea of a Jewish state, Israel as a Jewish state, that's in the charter. That's the way it is now. I do you support that? MAYOR ZOHRAN MAMDANI (D),
paragraphs.cn: 而犹太国家的概念，以色列作为犹太国家，这写在了宪章里。现在就是这样。你支持这一点吗？

paragraphs.speaker: New York City
paragraphs.en: I've said time and again that I support the state of Israel as a state with equal rights. I believe that any state that --
paragraphs.cn: 我一再表示，我支持以色列作为一个拥有平等权利的国家。我相信任何国家 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Karl
paragraphs.en: But as a Jewish state is the question.
paragraphs.cn: 但问题是作为犹太国家。

paragraphs.speaker: Mamdani
paragraphs.en: I think any state that privileges one religion over the other is one that I can't tell you I support, whether it be Israel or Saudi Arabia or anywhere else. And a lot of that comes back to a fundamental belief that we should all be considered equal, no matter what our faith is.
paragraphs.cn: 我认为任何优待某一宗教而非其他宗教的国家，我都无法说我支持，无论是以色列、沙特阿拉伯还是其他地方。这在很大程度上源于一个基本信念：无论我们的信仰是什么，我们都应该被视为平等的。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, this week, House Democratic leaders opposed an amendment that would block $3.3 billion in military aid to Israel. Leadership calls the measure flawed. Then you've got this growing progressive base that is pushing to break ranks. And if this amendment makes it to the floor, it could turn into a defining litmus test for the American left. So, I want to talk to you guys for a couple reasons.
paragraphs.cn: 所以，本周众议院民主党领导人反对一项阻止向以色列提供33亿美元军事援助的修正案。领导层称该措施有缺陷。然后，日益壮大的进步派基础正在推动打破常规。如果这项修正案进入表决阶段，它可能成为美国左翼的决定性试金石。因此，我想和你们谈谈，有几个原因。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: First, I was under the impression that Paul -- Thomas Massie was part of this amendment, which is somebody who just lost a race, very much blamed AIPAC, blamed groups lobbying on behalf of the -- of Israel.
paragraphs.cn: 首先，我原以为保罗 -- -- 托马斯·马西是这项修正案的一部分，他刚刚输掉了一场竞选，很大程度上归咎于AIPAC，归咎于代表以色列游说的团体。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Tucker Carlson starting to do -- you know, is doing this --
paragraphs.cn: 塔克·卡尔森开始 -- -- 你知道，正在做这个 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Tucker Carlson straight up left the party, right?
paragraphs.cn: 塔克·卡尔森直接退出了，对吧？

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Exactly. Well, over -- and a big part of it is over Israel. So, I don't think this is just happening on the Democrat side. It's happening on both sides. But more on the Democrat side. And I also think, listen, it scares me, the anti-Semitic undertones that are happening throughout the country. Not whether they're Israel. It's just that the racism in general, I would call it. And so, it does make me nervous that it's happening.
paragraphs.cn: 没错。嗯，很大程度上是因为以色列问题。所以，我认为这不仅发生在民主党一方。两方都有，但民主党方面更严重。而且，我也认为，听着，全国范围内出现的反犹太主义暗流让我感到害怕。不是针对以色列，而是我称之为普遍的种族主义。所以，这种情况的发生确实让我感到不安。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: But I think that this is going back to something like when you had all the Republicans say, if we win, we're never giving money to Ukraine. I think there is a lot of people that are just -- don't want Americans involved in other wars.
paragraphs.cn: 但我认为这又回到了类似的情况，比如所有共和党人都说，如果我们赢了，我们绝不会给乌克兰钱。我认为有很多人只是不想让美国人卷入其他战争。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. Well, I think that there is a way, though, of saying, pretty definitively, that after October 7th the conversation around Israel has changed tremendously. Just looking at Pew research surveys from March. Looking at the people who have somewhat unfavorable opinions of Israel. We wanted to look at it via age. And for people under the age of 50, on the Democratic side, we've heard this, 84 percent. On the Republican side, 57. OK. So, we're clearing the majority mark.
paragraphs.cn: 是的。不过，我认为有一种方式可以相当明确地说，10月7日之后，围绕以色列的讨论发生了巨大变化。看看3月份的皮尤研究调查。看看那些对以色列持相对负面看法的人。我们想按年龄来看。在50岁以下的民主党人中，我们听到这个比例是84%。在共和党人中是57%。所以，我们明显超过了多数。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And then ages 50 plus, it's not that much better, OK? So, the vote -- these lawmakers are appealing to someone. And then there's American's views on Israel in general, in 2022, 55 percent were favorable, in 2026, 37 percent. And in that context, it makes more sense for me to have been reading a headline back in June, in "Axios," where Trump was mad at Netanyahu, and he's yelling at him about Lebanon. But he doesn't just say that. He says, "you're f-ing crazy" and everyone hates you.
paragraphs.cn: 然后50岁以上的人群，情况也好不了多少，对吧？所以，这些议员是在迎合某些人。再看美国人对以色列的整体看法，2022年有55%的人持正面看法，2026年（应为2024年？原文如此）是37%。在这种背景下，我更能理解6月份在《Axios》上读到的一个头条新闻，特朗普对内塔尼亚胡很生气，就黎巴嫩问题对他大喊大叫。但他不只是说那些。他说："你他妈的疯了"，所有人都讨厌你。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And I thought that was a weird, tacit acknowledgment by the White House that the public sentiment is not in Netanyahu's favor.
paragraphs.cn: 我认为这是白宫一种奇怪的、默认的承认，即公众情绪并不支持内塔尼亚胡。

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.en: Well, I mean, I think President Trump is also frustrated with Netanyahu for other reasons because he's trying to get this peace agreement. He sees Netanyahu's strikes on Hezbollah in Lebanon as something that's, you know, helping to derail potentially a longer term agreement with Iran. So, there is a -- there's a few other things that are going on there.
paragraphs.cn: 嗯，我的意思是，我认为特朗普总统对内塔尼亚胡感到沮丧还有其他原因，因为他正试图达成和平协议。他认为内塔尼亚胡对黎巴嫩真主党的打击，你知道，可能有助于破坏与伊朗的长期协议。所以，这里还有其他一些事情在发生。

paragraphs.speaker: Chambers
paragraphs.en: But I wanted to pick up on what Ashley was saying about, I think this broader sentiment there is within the Democratic Party going back to October 7th in the 2024 election. We saw this with vice president, former Vice President Kamala Harris, you know, playing out over Israel. But you also see, within the Republican Party, this taking place on Israel.
paragraphs.cn: 但我想接着阿什利的话说，我认为民主党内部这种更广泛的情绪可以追溯到10月7日和2024年大选。我们看到副总统，前副总统卡玛拉·哈里斯在以色列问题上就是这样。但你也看到，在共和党内部，以色列问题上也在发生同样的事情。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: But can I go back to my point there --
paragraphs.cn: 但我能回到我的观点吗 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Because it's not -- I always joke that lawmakers are a lagging indicator. They're not at the forefront of what the public view is on Israel. They are followers. And so, are they looking at numbers and saying, wait a second, there's some vulnerability here. Maybe I should be speaking differently.
paragraphs.cn: 因为这不是 -- -- 我总是开玩笑说，立法者是滞后指标。他们不在公众对以色列看法的最前沿。他们是追随者。那么，他们是否在看着数字说，等等，这里有些脆弱性。也许我应该改变说法。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: I can answer this. It matters in the base of the left of the Democrats and in the base right of the Republicans. It's a very, very divisive issue. And to my point, 400 congressional seats are safe, Democrat or Republican. So, you can only be beat on this issue in your primary. In the general election, Audie, I promise you, I'm making these ads. We don't mention either one of these to a swing voter because it's not their number one issue.
paragraphs.cn: 我可以回答这个问题。这在民主党左翼基本盘和共和党右翼基本盘中都很重要。这是一个非常、非常分裂的问题。而且，正如我所说，400个国会席位是安全的，无论是民主党还是共和党。所以，你只会在初选中因为这个议题被击败。在大选中，奥迪，我向你保证，我在制作这些广告时，我们不会向摇摆选民提及其中任何一个，因为这不是他们的首要议题。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: They care about the price of everything in their life and not making enough money and they can't get ahead. Not saying it's not an important issue, but it's super important.
paragraphs.cn: 他们关心的是生活中所有东西的价格，赚不到足够的钱，无法进步。不是说这不重要，但它超级重要。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. I'm not saying that either, but I was showing you a poll of American's views, not extremist horseshoe views. We are talking a majority now.
paragraphs.cn: 是的。我也不是那个意思，但我给你看的是美国民众观点的民调，不是极端分子的马蹄形观点。我们现在说的是多数人。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: But those numbers, if you looked at the base of the party, the folks who actually vote, it's even tremendously more.
paragraphs.cn: 但这些数字，如果你看政党的基本盘，那些真正投票的人，甚至还要高得多。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: That's always code, the folks who actually vote.
paragraphs.cn: 这总是暗指那些真正投票的人。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I feel like that's a way of saying, there's some people who are excited now who aren't going to show up later.
paragraphs.cn: 我觉得这是一种说法，意思是有些现在兴奋的人以后不会出现。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: Y'all got to remember, it's about winning elections.
paragraphs.cn: 你们要记住，关键是要赢得选举。

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: Elected officials have access to different information than the American people of why a safe Israel, a strong Israel, is very important for our national security.
paragraphs.cn: 民选官员比美国民众更了解为什么一个安全的以色列、一个强大的以色列对我们的国家安全非常重要。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: And are they conveying this well enough to the voters?
paragraphs.cn: 他们是否向选民充分传达了这一点？

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: I don't think they can.
paragraphs.cn: 我认为他们做不到。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Or are they leaving this to the world of internet online personalities who will speak about it in more striking ways?
paragraphs.cn: 还是说，他们要把这个问题留给网络上的红人，让他们用更引人注目的方式去谈论？

paragraphs.speaker: Davis
paragraphs.en: They may be leaving it but -- they may be leaving it but I think that they'll take votes supporting Israel because how important -- they know how important it is. It's a hard (INAUDIBLE).
paragraphs.cn: 他们可能会回避，但 -- -- 他们可能会回避，但我认为他们会投票支持以色列，因为他们知道这有多重要。这很难（听不清）。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Well, this is going to come up more. We're actually going to have a guest on who can talk about this and other things. We're going to talk about why the party is evolving one way or another. And we're going to have live here a Michigan Senate candidate, who's going to talk about the party's future. Plus, President Trump calling out Republicans. Can Congress function without his approval? Group chat is back after the break.
paragraphs.cn: 好吧，这个问题还会被更多地提及。我们实际上会请一位嘉宾来谈论这个话题和其他事情。我们将讨论为什么这个政党正在以某种方式演变。我们还将现场连线一位密歇根州参议员候选人，他将谈论该党的未来。此外，特朗普总统正在批评共和党人。没有他的认可，国会能正常运作吗？群聊环节将在广告后回归。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, after recent primary victories by far-left, even Democratic socialist candidates in New York, the party is grappling with a surge that some have compared to the Tea Party movement Republicans faced 16 years ago. For some Democrats, that insurgent movement is not welcome.
paragraphs.cn: 在纽约州极左翼甚至民主社会主义候选人在最近的初选中获胜后，民主党正在应对一股浪潮，一些人将其与16年前共和党面临的茶党运动相提并论。对一些民主党人来说，这场叛乱运动并不受欢迎。

paragraphs.speaker: James Carville
paragraphs.en: I actually do think it's time for Democrats to talk the "s" word "schism." I really do. And everybody's always said, no, no, we -- we're coalition, we're big tent. And at some -- there's just some (EXPLETIVE DELETED) that I can't be in the same tent with.
paragraphs.cn: 我确实认为现在是民主党谈论"分裂"这个词的时候了。我真的这么认为。而每个人总是说，不，不，我们 -- -- 我们是联盟，我们是大帐篷。但在某些时候 -- -- 就是有一些（脏话已删除）让我无法和他们在同一个帐篷里。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, joining me now to discuss, Dr. Abdul El-Sayed. He is running for the Democratic nomination for the U.S. Senate in Michigan. Thank you so much for being here. We had one of your opponents on last week, so I'm glad you could come in. First, can we talk about the "s" word, as Carville describes it? ABDUL EL-SAYED (D),
paragraphs.cn: 现在，请加入我们讨论的是阿卜杜勒·埃尔-赛义德博士。他正在竞选密歇根州美国参议院席位的民主党提名。非常感谢你的到来。上周我们请了你的一个对手，所以我很高兴你能来。首先，我们能谈谈卡维尔所说的那个"分裂"这个词吗？阿卜杜勒·埃尔-赛义德（民主党）：

paragraphs.speaker: Michigan Senate Candidate
paragraphs.en: Yes. You know, it's funny, because isn't he the guy who said it's the economy, stupid. I think that's the "s" word we should be talking about. It is the economy. If you go to Michigan, if you go to New York, if you go anywhere in the country, people are frustrated because they can't afford the basic means of a dignified life.
paragraphs.cn: 是的。你知道，这很有趣，因为他不就是那个说"关键是经济，笨蛋"的人吗？我认为那才是我们应该谈论的"分裂"词。关键是经济。如果你去密歇根，如果你去纽约，如果你去美国的任何地方，人们都感到沮丧，因为他们负担不起体面生活的基本条件。

paragraphs.speaker: Michigan Senate Candidate
paragraphs.en: The fact that the Democrats have not delivered on those issues, the fact that people feel like both sides of our politics are not listening, that's why you're seeing a lot of these candidates, who are speaking differently about that economy, winning.
paragraphs.cn: 民主党没有在这些问题上取得成果，人们觉得政治双方都没有倾听，这就是为什么你会看到很多以不同方式谈论经济的候选人正在获胜。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Isn't his argument that it's not the economy part he disagrees with, it's everything else. It's the things we spoke about earlier, policy about Israel and how whether or not to support that government and its operations. Maybe it's around billionaire taxes. They have substantive questions and concerns about the Democratic socialist position.
paragraphs.cn: 他的论点难道不是他不同意的是经济部分，而是其他一切吗？是我们之前谈到的事情，关于以色列的政策，以及是否支持该政府及其行动。也许是关于亿万富翁税。他们对民主社会主义的立场有实质性的问题和担忧。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: I think one of the frustrations -- and, look, the real red scare we should be talking about is that Donald Trump is still president. Donald Trump is still doing things like taking us to wars we shouldn't be fighting. But I do want to step back and -- we do this thing of dissecting our politics as if all the things are not related. That you can talk about foreign policy, but you don't have to relate that to the economy.
paragraphs.cn: 我认为其中一个挫折是 -- -- 而且，看，我们真正应该谈论的红色恐慌是唐纳德·特朗普仍然是总统。唐纳德·特朗普仍在做一些事情，比如把我们拖入我们不应该打的战争。但我确实想退一步 -- -- 我们总是剖析我们的政治，好像所有事情都不相关。你可以谈论外交政策，但你不必将其与经济联系起来。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: Every dollar we send abroad to drop a bomb on another kid is a dollar we don't spend here to invest in your kids' school or your health care. The reason that our economy is rigged so that we have a trillionaire and the average person can't afford their basic life is because we have an economy where we've allowed big corporations to buy and sell politicians to rig the system against us. We --
paragraphs.cn: 我们每向国外寄出一美元去轰炸另一个孩子，就是少花一美元投资于你孩子的学校或你的医疗。我们的经济之所以被操纵，以至于我们有一个万亿富翁而普通人却负担不起基本生活，是因为我们允许大公司买卖政客来操纵体制与我们作对。我们 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, you're ready to talk economy without being accused of being a communist, or you're basically like, those labels don't matter anymore?
paragraphs.cn: 那么，你准备好谈论经济而不被指责为共产主义者了吗？或者你基本上觉得这些标签不再重要了？

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: Well, number one, I think people look askance when folks from D.C. call other people communists because we've heard that play run I don't know how many times. But the second point about that is, I just think it's important that we don't center parties in our politics. We center the people who vote for parties and vote in our politics. And I think the problem is that we're having this debate about where the party is going, but we're not actually talking about where people's lives are going.
paragraphs.cn: 嗯，第一，我认为当华盛顿的人称别人为共产主义者时，人们会侧目而视，因为我们听过这种把戏不知多少次了。但关于这一点，第二点是，我认为重要的是我们不要以政党为中心来搞政治。我们应以投票给政党并参与政治的人为中心。我认为问题在于我们正在辩论政党走向何方，但实际上并没有谈论人民的生活走向何方。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: And the politicians, the politics, that speaks to the lived experience of the average person that actually talks about how do we put money back in your pocket? How do we break the vice grip that corporations have on our politics?
paragraphs.cn: 而那些政客和政治，如果能够贴近普通人的实际生活，真正谈论如何把钱放回你的口袋？如何打破企业对政治的钳制？

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: How do we make sure that you can have health care? That's the kind of politics that's going to win. So, if you don't like where the party is going, then run an alternative that's actually going to address peoples' issues. But so long as you're having this nagging debate about where the party is going from your comfortable home in wherever it is, I just think you're missing the point because too many people are uncomfortable in their homes or don't have a home in the first place.
paragraphs.cn: 如何确保你能获得医疗？这才是能获胜的政治。所以，如果你不喜欢政党的走向，那就提出一个真正解决人民问题的替代方案。但只要你从你舒适的家中（无论在哪里）喋喋不休地争论政党走向，我认为你就错过了重点，因为太多人在自己的家中感到不适，或者根本没有家。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, your opponent, Mallory McMorrow, was on our show last week, as I mentioned. She was criticizing your decision to embrace the support of streamer Hasan Piker to represent your campaign. And what she said was very specific. That she goes, look, I have a problem with him embracing this person in the wake of an attack on a synagogue. This is when a truck was driven into the synagogue. I want to play for you what she said after that. MALLORY MCMORROW (D),
paragraphs.cn: 那么，你的对手马洛里·麦克莫罗上周上了我们的节目，正如我提到的。她批评你决定接受主播哈桑·派克的支持来代表你的竞选。她说的非常具体。她说，看，在犹太教堂遇袭之后，我对他接纳这个人有意见。当时一辆卡车冲进了犹太教堂。我想播放她之后说的话。马洛里·麦克莫罗（民主党）：

paragraphs.speaker: Michigan State Candidate
paragraphs.en: It could have been the largest mass killing of kids in this country's history. And to bring somebody in to represent your campaign, in my mind, is a very different decision than going on somebody's stream and talking to their audience.
paragraphs.cn: 这可能是美国历史上最大规模的儿童屠杀。而请一个人来代表你的竞选，在我看来，这与去某人的直播间和他们的观众交谈是非常不同的决定。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: I condemned the Temple Israel attack immediately. Any act of anti-Semitism is wrong and has no place in our politics, no place in our society. But I think what people find frustrating by that perspective is that, when you talk about the mass killing of children, we saw that done by our tax dollars in a genocide in Gaza. And it took, I don't know, two years to talk about that.
paragraphs.cn: 我立即谴责了针对以色列圣殿的袭击。任何反犹太主义行为都是错误的，在我们的政治中没有立足之地，在我们的社会中也没有立足之地。但我认为人们对这种观点感到沮丧的是，当你谈论大规模杀害儿童时，我们看到我们的税款在加沙的种族灭绝中做到了这一点。而我不知道花了两年时间才谈论这件事。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: And I think we have to take seriously the idea that the money that we spent to kill other people's kids there was money that we did not spend to take care of the kids that I was taking care of in Wayne County in Detroit to make sure they had --
paragraphs.cn: 我认为我们必须认真对待这样一个想法：我们花在杀死别人孩子上的钱，本可以用来照顾我在底特律韦恩县照顾的那些孩子，确保他们 -- --

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, no answer about this embrace of him. Do you think people are making too much or too little about this?
paragraphs.cn: 那么，关于接纳他，没有回答。你认为人们对这件事是反应过度还是反应不足？

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: Look, you know, it's a pretty crazy -- you know, so, Hasan Piker was invited to stream from the DNC in 2024. We had a whole debate about whether or not the vice president should have gone on Rogan. The idea that we're going to tie people together and say, well, guilt by association so is just not in keeping with the way most of us think about the world.
paragraphs.cn: 你看，这挺疯狂的 -- -- 你知道，哈桑·派克受邀在2024年民主党全国代表大会上直播。我们曾就副总统是否应该上罗根的节目进行过一场大辩论。那种把人们捆绑在一起，说'有罪关联'的想法，根本不符合我们大多数人对世界的看法。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: I'm sitting on a panel with a very diverse group of people. Did I vet all of you before I came and sat here because one of you might have said something I disagree with? I just think it's a ridiculous way to think about politics.
paragraphs.cn: 我现在和一个非常多元化的群体坐在一起讨论。我来之前是不是应该把你们所有人都审查一遍，因为你们中可能有人说过我不同意的话？我只是觉得这是一种荒谬的政治思维方式。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Yes. Well, you're running for office, which is why you're in the hot seat.
paragraphs.cn: 是的。但你在竞选公职，这就是为什么你处于风口浪尖。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: Well, I'm happy to be in the hot seat, but let me just speak to that.
paragraphs.cn: 好吧，我很乐意处于风口浪尖，但请允许我对此说几句。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: But this is why you're being vetted. I want to raise something. You used the word "genocide."
paragraphs.cn: 但这就是你被审查的原因。我想提一件事。你用了'种族灭绝'这个词。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: This is the kind of thing that people point to over and over again as a problem for a party that is trying to zip up its big tent.
paragraphs.cn: 这正是人们反复指出的问题，对于一个试图扩大包容性的政党来说，这是个问题。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: "The Detroit Free Press" editorial board just endorsed Mallory McMorrow and said that you are "running a divisive campaign, casting" your "opponents as morally compromised."
paragraphs.cn: 《底特律自由报》编辑委员会刚刚支持了马洛里·麦克莫罗，并说你'正在进行一场分裂性的竞选'，将你的对手描绘成'道德上有缺陷'的人。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: I think if you've taken money from the people who caused the problems in your life, and then come and tell people, hey, we're going to solve your problems, that's just inconsistent with being serious about solving problems. Now, if that's divisive, I think we need to have hard conversations in our party. I think we need to be talking about who's actually there to solve problems for you.
paragraphs.cn: 我认为，如果你从那些给你生活造成问题的人那里拿了钱，然后跑来告诉人们'嘿，我们要解决你们的问题'，那这与认真解决问题的态度是不一致的。如果这算是分裂，那么我认为我们党内需要进行艰难的对话。我们需要讨论谁才是真正为你解决问题的人。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: If you're not serious about not taking money from the utility company as people's rates are going up, if you're not serious about not taking money from the health insurance corporations who just raised your premiums 15 percent and putting millions of people in bankruptcy, I'm so sorry, I don't think you're serious about solving the problems. If that makes me divisive, that's fine. But look, the American public is going to get to pick. The Michiganders are going to get to pick.
paragraphs.cn: 如果你在人们电费上涨时，不认真对待不从电力公司拿钱的问题；如果你在健康保险公司刚把保费提高15%、让数百万人破产时，不认真对待不从他们那里拿钱的问题，那么我很抱歉，我不认为你是在认真解决问题。如果这让我显得分裂，那没关系。但你看，美国公众会做出选择。密歇根州的人民会做出选择。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: And whether we're talking about the parties writ large or we're talking about our Michigan race, it's on people to get to decide.
paragraphs.cn: 无论我们谈论的是整个政党，还是我们密歇根州的竞选，最终都由人民来决定。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: And this is just what I'm saying. This is not a conversation about parties. So long as we are missing the conversation about everyday people and why they are struggling in their lives, we are missing the point of Democratic politics in the first place.
paragraphs.cn: 这正是我要说的。这不是关于党派的对话。只要我们缺失了关于普通民众以及他们为何在生活中挣扎的对话，我们就首先错过了民主政治的重点。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: OK, I want you guys -- do you have a question, actually? CHUCK ROCHA, FORMER SENIOR ADVISER TO BERNIE SANDERS' 2016 AND 2020
paragraphs.cn: 好的，我想让你们 -- -- 你们有问题吗？查克·罗查，伯尼·桑德斯2016年和2020年竞选活动的前高级顾问。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: In Michigan, you know, it's a really unique place because of the auto industry and because of an influx of immigrants, lots of Latinos from south Texas, a lot of folks from lots of parts of the country have come in there.
paragraphs.cn: 在密歇根州，你知道，那是一个非常独特的地方，因为汽车工业，也因为移民的涌入，很多来自南德克萨斯的拉丁裔，很多来自全国各地的人都来到了那里。

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: As the last working class consultant who never went to college, who worked for the steelworkers union, who have watched my brothers and sisters who grew up there like I did with a single mom trying to figure it out, none of them talked to me every day about the issues that are debated on TV like y'all just finished doing. They're talking to me about things costing too much and not making enough money. Most folks haven't seen real wage gains in years.
paragraphs.cn: 作为最后一个从未上过大学、为钢铁工人工会工作、看着像我一样由单亲妈妈抚养长大的兄弟姐妹们努力谋生的工人阶级顾问，他们中没有一个人每天跟我谈论像你们刚才那样在电视上辩论的问题。他们跟我谈论的是东西太贵、赚得不够多。大多数人多年来都没有看到实际工资的增长。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Wait, Chuck, is this a question?
paragraphs.cn: 等等，查克，这是个问题吗？

paragraphs.speaker: Rocha
paragraphs.en: Yes. What are you seeing out there between you and the other candidates and what those people are saying? That's all I care about.
paragraphs.cn: 是的。你在外面看到你和别的候选人之间的情况，以及那些人说了什么？我只关心这个。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: Chuck, thank you for saying that. I'm honored to have earned the UAW endorsement. And look, my dad came to America to build cars. But you talk to autoworkers out in Michigan, Macomb County, in Detroit, and they'll tell you, look, my life has gotten harder to afford every single year for the past ten years. Doesn't matter who's president. Every year I struggle. And they keep telling me that I'm not going to be able to get further. And I'm watching Elon Musk become a trillionaire.
paragraphs.cn: 查克，谢谢你这么说。我很荣幸获得了美国汽车工人联合会的支持。你看，我父亲来美国是为了造车。但你去和密歇根州、马科姆县、底特律的汽车工人聊聊，他们会告诉你，看，过去十年里，我的生活每年都变得更难负担。不管谁是总统。每年我都在挣扎。他们一直告诉我，我无法更进一步。而我看着埃隆·马斯克成为万亿富翁。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Do you feel like you would enter a Congress and be able to work in a bipartisan way how -- given how you've spoken about these folks, not just in the party, but across?
paragraphs.cn: 你觉得你进入国会后，能够以两党合作的方式工作吗 -- -- 考虑到你刚才谈论这些人的方式，不仅是在党内，而是跨党派？

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: I'll work -- I'll work with anyone who wants to work with me to get things done. But I'll also tell you this, I'm not going to pretend like not calling out the problems that I see is going to make things better.
paragraphs.cn: 我会和任何愿意与我合作的人一起工作，把事情办成。但我也要告诉你，我不会假装不指出我看到的问题就能让事情变得更好。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: The real conversation is not between me and 99 other senators when I get there, it's between me and the 350 million people. It's between all of us and the 350 million people who elect all of us. And that's the mistake that I think both parties are making. We're not having a conversation with the actual public, we're out here debating these issues and we're watching as this discussion is getting further and further away from the lived experience of everyday people.
paragraphs.cn: 真正的对话不是我和其他99位参议员之间的，而是我和3.5亿人民之间的。是我们所有人与选举我们的3.5亿人民之间的对话。我认为这是两党都在犯的错误。我们没有与真正的公众对话，我们在这里辩论这些问题，看着这场讨论离普通民众的生活经验越来越远。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: One issue I care about a lot on this show is A.I. and its effect on the economy to come. You have talked about public takeover, if I have that right, or privatization.
paragraphs.cn: 在这个节目中，我非常关心的一个问题是人工智能及其对未来经济的影响。你曾提到过公有化，如果我没理解错的话，或者是私有化。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: Takeover was not the word. If you want to use that word.
paragraphs.cn: 不是"接管"这个词。如果你想用那个词的话。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Oh, what word do you want to use.
paragraphs.cn: 哦，那你想用什么词？

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: A.I. under democracy. I mean democracy is, like, you know, public takeover of government.
paragraphs.cn: 民主下的人工智能。我的意思是，民主就是，你知道，公众接管政府。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Who would run the company, the government or the people who own the company?
paragraphs.cn: 谁来运营公司，政府还是公司的所有者？

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: I think that if you have a technology that is going to vastly change the nature of the economy, it's really important that you have democratic oversight of that technology. So, if we call that a takeover, then we've had a takeover of a lot of different things. But I think democracy is a good thing. And A.I. is a scary new technology, while democracy is a very tried and true old technology. And I'm just saying that you should have democracy overseeing the way A.I. is being deployed.
paragraphs.cn: 我认为，如果一项技术将极大地改变经济本质，那么对该技术进行民主监督就非常重要。所以，如果我们称之为接管，那么我们已经接管了很多不同的东西。但我认为民主是好事。人工智能是一项可怕的新技术，而民主是一项久经考验的旧技术。我只是说，应该让民主来监督人工智能的部署方式。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Well, another litmus test then. Would you turn down money from A.I. companies, and do you think other Democrats should?
paragraphs.cn: 那么，另一个试金石。你会拒绝来自人工智能公司的捐款吗？你认为其他民主党人也应该这样做吗？

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: I turn -- I turn down money from all companies. I don't take corporate money.
paragraphs.cn: 我拒绝 -- -- 我拒绝所有公司的捐款。我不接受企业资金。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Have you turned down money from an A.I. company?
paragraphs.cn: 你拒绝过人工智能公司的捐款吗？

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: I've taken -- turned down money from every company. I don't take corporate money.
paragraphs.cn: 我拒绝过 -- -- 拒绝过所有公司的捐款。我不接受企业资金。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: Should Democrats turn down money from A.I. companies?
paragraphs.cn: 民主党人应该拒绝人工智能公司的捐款吗？

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: I think Democrats should turn down money from all companies, because you should not be bought by the people who have interests in making sure government works for them instead of working for everyday people. This is a simple thing.
paragraphs.cn: 我认为民主党人应该拒绝所有公司的捐款，因为你不应该被那些希望政府为他们服务而不是为普通民众服务的人收买。这很简单。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: So, you've got a race coming up, August 4th. You going to spend more time back in state, or are you going to be out here talking more to the general voter?
paragraphs.cn: 那么，8月4日即将有一场竞选。你会花更多时间回到本州，还是会在外面更多地向普通选民发表讲话？

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: Well, I'll be out here talking to folks, but I spend almost all my time in Michigan. But since I was here, I figured I'd come and talk to you.
paragraphs.cn: 嗯，我会在外面和人们交谈，但我几乎所有时间都在密歇根州。不过既然我在这里，我想我可以来和你聊聊。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: I appreciate it, because you're an epidemiologist by trade, and y'all had a rough decade. I don't know any public health folks who have come out unscathed.
paragraphs.cn: 我很感激，因为你是流行病学家出身，而你们这十年过得很艰难。我认识的公共卫生领域的人没有一个能毫发无损地走出来。

paragraphs.speaker: El-Sayed
paragraphs.en: I would tell you, I think we, as a country, had a rough decade. And we were trying to fix it, address it. And, you know, as an epidemiologist, I'm trained to understand problems as I see them and to try to solve them. So, I hope that I can apply that in our politics as well.
paragraphs.cn: 我想告诉你，我认为我们作为一个国家，这十年过得很艰难。我们一直在努力修复它，应对它。而且，作为一名流行病学家，我受过训练，能够理解我所看到的问题，并试图解决它们。所以，我希望我也能将这一点应用到我们的政治中。

paragraphs.speaker: Cornish
paragraphs.en: OK. Dr. Abdul El-Sayed, thank you so much for being here. And thank you all for being in this conversation with us. We know you could be in a lot of places and we're glad you're here with us. I'm Audie Cornish, and the headlines are next. var CNN = CNN || {};
paragraphs.cn: 好的。阿卜杜勒·埃尔-赛义德博士，非常感谢你来到这里。也感谢大家参与我们的对话。我们知道你们本可以去很多地方，但我们很高兴你们在这里。我是奥蒂·科尼什，接下来是新闻提要。

vocabulary.cn: 出生公民权（在美国出生即自动获得公民身份的权利）
vocabulary.en: the right to citizenship for anyone born in a country's territory
vocabulary.excerpt: Expected among those rulings, a key one on birthright citizenship
vocabulary.exampleCn: 预计这些裁决中，有一项关于出生公民权的关键裁决。

vocabulary.cn: 行政命令（总统发布的具有法律效力的指令）
vocabulary.en: a rule or order issued by the president to the executive branch
vocabulary.excerpt: the president tried to end with an executive order on his first day in office
vocabulary.exampleCn: 总统在上任第一天试图通过行政命令终止它。

vocabulary.cn: 邮寄选票（通过邮寄方式投递的选票）
vocabulary.en: a ballot that is mailed to voters and returned by mail
vocabulary.excerpt: something like mail-in ballots didn't quite work out
vocabulary.exampleCn: 像邮寄选票这样的事情并不太顺利。

vocabulary.cn: 美联储（美国中央银行系统）
vocabulary.en: the central banking system of the United States
vocabulary.excerpt: the court was able to treat the Federal Reserve different from other agencies
vocabulary.exampleCn: 法院能够将美联储与其他机构区别对待。

vocabulary.en: to cause something to be upside down or to disrupt completely
vocabulary.excerpt: the Fed has the power to upend global markets
vocabulary.exampleCn: 美联储有能力颠覆全球市场。

vocabulary.cn: 异议（尤指法官的不同意见）
vocabulary.en: a statement by a judge disagreeing with the majority opinion
vocabulary.excerpt: Amy Coney Barrett noted in a dissent that's kind of bonkers
vocabulary.exampleCn: 艾米·科尼·巴雷特在异议中指出这有点疯狂。

vocabulary.en: fraud involving mortgage loans, such as falsifying information
vocabulary.excerpt: she was accused of some mortgage fraud and irregularities
vocabulary.exampleCn: 她被指控存在抵押贷款欺诈和违规行为。

vocabulary.en: the act of ending something, especially employment
vocabulary.excerpt: her termination was related to that
vocabulary.exampleCn: 她的解雇与此有关。

vocabulary.en: claimed or alleged, often without proof
vocabulary.excerpt: her purported termination
vocabulary.exampleCn: 她据称被解雇。

vocabulary.en: to be in agreement with; to match
